question:
Let $\mathbf{S} = \{(a,b) | a = 1, 2, \dots,n, b = 1,2,3\}$.
A \emph{rook tour} of $\mathbf{S}$ is a polygonal path made up of line
segments connecting points $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_{3n}$ in sequence such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $p_i \in \mathbf{S}$,
\item[(ii)] $p_i$ and $p_{i+1}$ are a unit distance apart, for
$1 \leq i <3n$,
\item[(iii)] for each $p \in \mathbf{S}$ there is a unique $i$ such that
$p_i = p$. How many rook tours are there that begin at $(1,1)$
and end at $(n,1)$?
\end{enumerate}
(An example of such a rook tour for $n=5$ was depicted in the original.)
solution:
We will assume $n \geq 2$ hereafter, since the answer is 0 for $n=1$.

\textbf{First solution:}
We show that the set of rook tours from $(1,1)$ to $(n,1)$ is in bijection with
the set of subsets of $\{1,2,...,n\}$ that include $n$ and contain an even number
of elements in total.  Since the latter set evidently contains $2^{n-2}$ elements,
so does the former.

We now construct the bijection.  Given a rook tour $P$ from $(1,1)$ to $(n,1)$,
let $S=S(P)$ denote the set of all $i \in \{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ for which there is
either a directed edge from $(i,1)$ to $(i,2)$ or from $(i,3)$ to $(i,2)$.  It
is clear that this set $S$ includes $n$ and must contain an even number of
elements.  Conversely, given a subset $S=\{a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_{2r}=n\}
\subset \{1,2,\ldots,n\}$ of this type with $a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_{2r}$,
we notice that there is a unique path $P$ containing
$(a_i,2+(-1)^i),(a_1,2)$
for $i=1,2,\ldots,2r$.  This establishes the desired bijection.


\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $A_n$ denote the set of rook tours beginning at $(1,1)$ and
ending at $(n,1)$, and let $B_n$ denote the set of rook tours
beginning at $(1,1)$ and ending at $(n,3)$.

For $n \geq 2$, we construct a bijection between $A_n$ and $A_{n-1}
\cup B_{n-1}$. Any path $P$ in $A_n$ contains either the line segment
$P_1$ between $(n-1,1)$ and $(n,1)$, or the line segment $P_2$ between
$(n,2)$ and $(n,1)$. In the former case, $P$ must also contain the
subpath $P_1'$ which joins $(n-1,3)$, $(n,3)$, $(n,2)$, and $(n-1,2)$
consecutively; then deleting $P_1$ and $P_1'$ from $P$ and adding the
line segment joining $(n-1,3)$ to $(n-1,2)$ results in a path in
$A_{n-1}$. (This construction is reversible, lengthening any path in
$A_{n-1}$ to a path in $A_n$.) In the latter case, $P$ contains the
subpath $P_2'$ which joins $(n-1,3)$, $(n,3)$, $(n,2)$, $(n,1)$
consecutively; deleting $P_2'$ results in a path in $B_{n-1}$, and
this construction is also reversible. The desired bijection follows.

Similarly, there is a bijection between $B_n$ and $A_{n-1} \cup
B_{n-1}$ for $n \geq 2$. It follows by induction that for $n \geq 2$,
$|A_n| = |B_n| = 2^{n-2} (|A_1| + |B_1|)$. But $|A_1| = 0$ and $|B_1|
= 1$, and hence the desired answer is $|A_n| = 2^{n-2}$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
Other bijective arguments are possible: for instance, Noam Elkies
points out that each element of $A_n \cup B_n$
contains a different one of the possible sets of
segments of the form $(i,2),(i+1,2)$ for $i=1,\dots,n-1$.
Richard Stanley provides the reference: K.L. Collins and L.B. Krompart,
The number of Hamiltonian paths in a rectangular grid,
\textit{Discrete Math.}  \textbf{169} (1997), 29--38. This problem
is Theorem~1 of that paper; the cases of $4 \times n$ and $5 \times n$
grids
are also treated. The paper can also be found online at the URL
\texttt{kcollins.web.wesleyan.edu/vita.htm}.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $\mathbf{toursubset} = \{(horizix,vertix) \mid horizix = 1, 2, \dots,boardsize, \; vertix = 1,2,3\}$.  
A \emph{rook tour} of $\mathbf{toursubset}$ is a polygonal path made up of line
segments connecting points $tourpt_1, tourpt_2, \dots, tourpt_{3boardsize}$ in sequence such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] $tourpt_i \in \mathbf{toursubset}$,
\item[(ii)] $tourpt_i$ and $tourpt_{i+1}$ are a unit distance apart, for
$1 \le i < 3boardsize$,
\item[(iii)] for each $tourpt \in \mathbf{toursubset}$ there is a unique $i$ such that $tourpt_i = tourpt$.
How many rook tours are there that begin at $(1,1)$ and end at $(boardsize,1)$?
\end{enumerate}
(An example of such a rook tour for $boardsize=5$ was depicted in the original.)
descriptive_long.solution:
We will assume $boardsize \ge 2$ hereafter, since the answer is $0$ for $boardsize=1$.

\textbf{First solution:}
We show that the set of rook tours from $(1,1)$ to $(boardsize,1)$ is in bijection with the set of subsets of $\{1,2,\dots,boardsize\}$ that include $boardsize$ and contain an even number of elements in total.  Since the latter set evidently contains $2^{boardsize-2}$ elements, so does the former.

We now construct the bijection.  Given a rook tour $rooktour$ from $(1,1)$ to $(boardsize,1)$, let $\toursubset = \toursubset(rooktour)$ denote the set of all $i \in \{1,2,\ldots,boardsize\}$ for which there is either a directed edge from $(i,1)$ to $(i,2)$ or from $(i,3)$ to $(i,2)$.  It is clear that this set $\toursubset$ includes $boardsize$ and must contain an even number of elements.  Conversely, given a subset
\[
\toursubset = \{subsetel_1, subsetel_2, \ldots, subsetel_{2paircount}=boardsize\} \subset \{1,2,\ldots,boardsize\}
\]
of this type with $subsetel_1 < subsetel_2 < \cdots < subsetel_{2paircount}$, we notice that there is a unique path $rooktour$ containing the points $(subsetel_i, 2+(-1)^i)$ and $(subsetel_1,2)$ for $i = 1,2,\ldots,2paircount$.  This establishes the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let $tourseta_{boardsize}$ denote the set of rook tours beginning at $(1,1)$ and ending at $(boardsize,1)$, and let $toursetb_{boardsize}$ denote the set of rook tours beginning at $(1,1)$ and ending at $(boardsize,3)$.

For $boardsize \ge 2$, we construct a bijection between $tourseta_{boardsize}$ and $tourseta_{boardsize-1} \cup toursetb_{boardsize-1}$.  Any path $rooktour$ in $tourseta_{boardsize}$ contains either the line segment $rooksegmentone$ between $(boardsize-1,1)$ and $(boardsize,1)$, or the line segment $rooksegmenttwo$ between $(boardsize,2)$ and $(boardsize,1)$.  In the former case, $rooktour$ must also contain the subpath $rooksegmentone'$ which joins $(boardsize-1,3)$, $(boardsize,3)$, $(boardsize,2)$, and $(boardsize-1,2)$ consecutively; deleting $rooksegmentone$ and $rooksegmentone'$ from $rooktour$ and adding the line segment joining $(boardsize-1,3)$ to $(boardsize-1,2)$ results in a path in $tourseta_{boardsize-1}$.  (This construction is reversible, lengthening any path in $tourseta_{boardsize-1}$ to a path in $tourseta_{boardsize}$.)  In the latter case, $rooktour$ contains the subpath $rooksegmenttwo'$ which joins $(boardsize-1,3)$, $(boardsize,3)$, $(boardsize,2)$, $(boardsize,1)$ consecutively; deleting $rooksegmenttwo'$ results in a path in $toursetb_{boardsize-1}$, and this construction is also reversible.  The desired bijection follows.

Similarly, there is a bijection between $toursetb_{boardsize}$ and $tourseta_{boardsize-1} \cup toursetb_{boardsize-1}$ for $boardsize \ge 2$.  It follows by induction that for $boardsize \ge 2$, $|tourseta_{boardsize}| = |toursetb_{boardsize}| = 2^{boardsize-2} (|tourseta_1| + |toursetb_1|)$.  But $|tourseta_1| = 0$ and $|toursetb_1| = 1$, and hence the desired answer is $|tourseta_{boardsize}| = 2^{boardsize-2}$.

\textbf{Remarks:} Other bijective arguments are possible: for instance, Noam Elkies points out that each element of $tourseta_{boardsize} \cup toursetb_{boardsize}$ contains a different one of the possible sets of segments of the form $(i,2),(i+1,2)$ for $i = 1,\dots,boardsize-1$.  Richard Stanley provides the reference: K.L. Collins and L.B. Krompart, \emph{The number of Hamiltonian paths in a rectangular grid}, Discrete Math. \textbf{169} (1997), 29--38.  This problem is Theorem~1 of that paper; the cases of $4 \times boardsize$ and $5 \times boardsize$ grids are also treated.  The paper can also be found online at the URL \texttt{kcollins.web.wesleyan.edu/vita.htm}.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $\mathbf{astronaut} = \{(lanterns,monolith) | lanterns = 1, 2, \dots,horizons, monolith = 1,2,3\}$.
A \emph{rook tour} of $\mathbf{astronaut}$ is a polygonal path made up of line
segments connecting points $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_{3horizons}$ in sequence such that
 egin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] harpooneri $\in \mathbf{astronaut}$,
\item[(ii)] harpooneri and $p_{i+1}$ are a unit distance apart, for
$1 \leq i <3horizons$,
\item[(iii)] for each harpooner $\in \mathbf{astronaut}$ there is a unique $i$ such that
harpooneri = harpooner. How many rook tours are there that begin at $(1,1)$
and end at $(horizons,1)$?
\end{enumerate}
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We will assume $horizons \geq 2$ hereafter, since the answer is 0 for $horizons=1$.

	extbf{First solution:}
We show that the set of rook tours from $(1,1)$ to $(horizons,1)$ is in bijection with
the set of subsets of $\{1,2,...,horizons\}$ that include horizons and contain an even number
of elements in total.  Since the latter set evidently contains $2^{horizons-2}$ elements,
so does the former.

We now construct the bijection.  Given a rook tour quartzite from $(1,1)$ to $(horizons,1)$,
let $astronaut=astronaut(quartzite)$ denote the set of all $i \in \{1,2,\ldots,horizons\}$ for which there is
either a directed edge from $(i,1)$ to $(i,2)$ or from $(i,3)$ to $(i,2)$.  It
is clear that this set astronaut includes horizons and must contain an even number of
elements.  Conversely, given a subset $astronaut=\{lanterns_1,lanterns_2,\ldots,lanterns_{2driftwood}=horizons\}
\subset \{1,2,\ldots,horizons\}$ of this type with $lanterns_1<lanterns_2<\cdots<lanterns_{2driftwood}$,
we notice that there is a unique path quartzite containing
$(lanterns_i,2+(-1)^i),(lanterns_1,2)$
for $i=1,2,\ldots,2driftwood$.  This establishes the desired bijection.

	extbf{Second solution:}
Let wilderness denote the set of rook tours beginning at $(1,1)$ and
ending at $(horizons,1)$, and let lumberjack denote the set of rook tours
beginning at $(1,1)$ and ending at $(horizons,3)$.

For horizons $\geq 2$, we construct a bijection between wilderness and $A_{horizons-1}
\cup B_{horizons-1}$. Any path quartzite in wilderness contains either the line segment
hemlocken between $(horizons-1,1)$ and $(horizons,1)$, or the line segment graphitem between
$(horizons,2)$ and $(horizons,1)$. In the former case, quartzite must also contain the
subpath $hemlocken'$ which joins $(horizons-1,3)$, $(horizons,3)$, $(horizons,2)$, and $(horizons-1,2)$
consecutively; then deleting hemlocken and $hemlocken'$ from quartzite and adding the
line segment joining $(horizons-1,3)$ to $(horizons-1,2)$ results in a path in
$A_{horizons-1}$. (This construction is reversible, lengthening any path in
$A_{horizons-1}$ to a path in wilderness.) In the latter case, quartzite contains the
subpath $graphitem'$ which joins $(horizons-1,3)$, $(horizons,3)$, $(horizons,2)$, $(horizons,1)$
consecutively; deleting $graphitem'$ results in a path in $B_{horizons-1}$, and
this construction is also reversible. The desired bijection follows.

Similarly, there is a bijection between lumberjack and $A_{horizons-1} \cup
B_{horizons-1}$ for horizons $\geq 2$. It follows by induction that for horizons $\geq 2$,
$|wilderness| = |lumberjack| = 2^{horizons-2} (|A_1| + |B_1|)$. But $|A_1| = 0$ and $|B_1|
= 1$, and hence the desired answer is $|wilderness| = 2^{horizons-2}$.

	extbf{Remarks:}
Other bijective arguments are possible: for instance, Noam Elkies
points out that each element of wilderness $\cup$ lumberjack
contains a different one of the possible sets of
segments of the form $(i,2),(i+1,2)$ for $i=1,\dots,horizons-1$.
Richard Stanley provides the reference: K.L. Collins and L.B. Krompart,
The number of Hamiltonian paths in a rectangular grid,
	extit{Discrete Math.}  	extbf{169} (1997), 29--38. This problem
is Theorem~1 of that paper; the cases of $4 	imes horizons$ and $5 	imes horizons$
grids
are also treated. The paper can also be found online at the URL
	exttt{kcollins.web.wesleyan.edu/vita.htm}.}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $\mathbf{emptiness} = \{(lastletter,firstnumber) | lastletter = 1, 2, \dots,infinitum, firstnumber = 1,2,3\}.$
A \emph{rook tour} of $\mathbf{emptiness}$ is a polygonal path made up of line
segments connecting points $vastspace_1, vastspace_2, \dots, vastspace_{3infinitum}$ in sequence such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(i)] planesection $\in \mathbf{emptiness}$,
\item[(ii)] planesection and $vastspace_{i+1}$ are a unit distance apart, for
$1 \leq i <3infinitum$,
\item[(iii)] for each vastspace $\in \mathbf{emptiness}$ there is a unique $i$ such that
planesection = vastspace.
How many rook tours are there that begin at $(1,1)$ and end at $(infinitum,1)$?
\end{enumerate}
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We will assume $infinitum \geq 2$ hereafter, since the answer is 0 for $infinitum=1$.

\textbf{First solution:}
We show that the set of rook tours from $(1,1)$ to $(infinitum,1)$ is in bijection with
the set of subsets of $\{1,2,...,infinitum\}$ that include infinitum and contain an even number
of elements in total.  Since the latter set evidently contains $2^{infinitum-2}$ elements,
so does the former.

We now construct the bijection.  Given a rook tour stillness from $(1,1)$ to $(infinitum,1)$,
let emptiness=emptiness(stillness) denote the set of all $i \in \{1,2,\ldots,infinitum\}$ for which there is
either a directed edge from $(i,1)$ to $(i,2)$ or from $(i,3)$ to $(i,2)$.  It
is clear that this set emptiness includes infinitum and must contain an even number of
elements.  Conversely, given a subset emptiness=$\{lateindex_1,lateindex_2,\ldots,lateindex_{2stagnant}=infinitum\}
\subset \{1,2,\ldots,infinitum\}$ of this type with $lateindex_1<lateindex_2<\cdots<lateindex_{2stagnant}$,
we notice that there is a unique path stillness containing
$(lateindex_i,2+(-1)^i),(lateindex_1,2)$
for $i=1,2,\ldots,2stagnant$.  This establishes the desired bijection.

\textbf{Second solution:}
Let zeropaths denote the set of rook tours beginning at $(1,1)$ and
ending at $(infinitum,1)$, and let fullpaths denote the set of rook tours
beginning at $(1,1)$ and ending at $(infinitum,3)$.

For $infinitum \geq 2$, we construct a bijection between zeropaths and $A_{infinitum-1}
\cup B_{infinitum-1}$. Any path stillness in zeropaths contains either the line segment
segmentone between $(infinitum-1,1)$ and $(infinitum,1)$, or the line segment segmenttwo between
$(infinitum,2)$ and $(infinitum,1)$. In the former case, stillness must also contain the
subpath $P_1'$ which joins $(infinitum-1,3)$, $(infinitum,3)$, $(infinitum,2)$, and $(infinitum-1,2)$
consecutively; then deleting segmentone and $P_1'$ from stillness and adding the
line segment joining $(infinitum-1,3)$ to $(infinitum-1,2)$ results in a path in
$A_{infinitum-1}$. (This construction is reversible, lengthening any path in
$A_{infinitum-1}$ to a path in zeropaths.) In the latter case, stillness contains the
subpath $P_2'$ which joins $(infinitum-1,3)$, $(infinitum,3)$, $(infinitum,2)$, $(infinitum,1)$
consecutively; deleting $P_2'$ results in a path in $B_{infinitum-1}$, and
this construction is also reversible. The desired bijection follows.

Similarly, there is a bijection between fullpaths and $A_{infinitum-1} \cup
B_{infinitum-1}$ for $infinitum \geq 2$. It follows by induction that for $infinitum \geq 2$,
$|zeropaths| = |fullpaths| = 2^{infinitum-2} (|A_1| + |B_1|)$. But $|A_1| = 0$ and $|B_1|
= 1$, and hence the desired answer is $|zeropaths| = 2^{infinitum-2}$.

\textbf{Remarks:}
Other bijective arguments are possible: for instance, Noam Elkies
points out that each element of zeropaths $\cup$ fullpaths
contains a different one of the possible sets of
segments of the form $(i,2),(i+1,2)$ for $i=1,\dots,infinitum-1$.
Richard Stanley provides the reference: K.L. Collins and L.B. Krompart,
The number of Hamiltonian paths in a rectangular grid,
\textit{Discrete Math.}  \textbf{169} (1997), 29--38. This problem
is Theorem~1 of that paper; the cases of $4 \times infinitum$ and $5 \times infinitum$
grids
are also treated. The paper can also be found online at the URL
\texttt{kcollins.web.wesleyan.edu/vita.htm}.

garbled_string.question: Let $\mathbf{ndzfpwro} = \{(qzxwvtnp,hjgrksla) | qzxwvtnp = 1, 2, \dots,ftgzlosy, hjgrksla = 1,2,3\}$. A \emph{rook tour} of $\mathbf{ndzfpwro}$ is a polygonal path made up of line segments connecting points $lkmqzvce_1, lkmqzvce_2, \dots, lkmqzvce_{3ftgzlosy}$ in sequence such that \begin{enumerate} \item[(i)] $vtrsjbqa \in \mathbf{ndzfpwro}$, \item[(ii)] $vtrsjbqa$ and $lkmqzvce_{i+1}$ are a unit distance apart, for $1 \leq i <3ftgzlosy$, \item[(iii)] for each $lkmqzvce \in \mathbf{ndzfpwro}$ there is a unique $i$ such that $vtrsjbqa = lkmqzvce$. How many rook tours are there that begin at $(1,1)$ and end at $(ftgzlosy,1)$? \end{enumerate} (An example of such a rook tour for $ftgzlosy=5$ was depicted in the original.)
garbled_string.solution:
We will assume $ftgzlosy \geq 2$ hereafter, since the answer is 0 for $ftgzlosy=1$.\par
oindent	extbf{First solution:} We show that the set of rook tours from $(1,1)$ to $(ftgzlosy,1)$ is in bijection with the set of subsets of $\{1,2,...,ftgzlosy\}$ that include $ftgzlosy$ and contain an even number of elements in total.  Since the latter set evidently contains $2^{ftgzlosy-2}$ elements, so does the former.\par We now construct the bijection.  Given a rook tour ofiubnzr from $(1,1)$ to $(ftgzlosy,1)$, let ndzfpwro=ndzfpwro(ofiubnzr) denote the set of all $i \in \{1,2,\ldots,ftgzlosy\}$ for which there is either a directed edge from $(i,1)$ to $(i,2)$ or from $(i,3)$ to $(i,2)$.  It is clear that this set ndzfpwro includes $ftgzlosy$ and must contain an even number of elements.  Conversely, given a subset ndzfpwro=\{zrnqylcd_1,zrnqylcd_2,\ldots,zrnqylcd_{2wpsdhjkv}=ftgzlosy\} \subset \{1,2,\ldots,ftgzlosy\}$ of this type with $zrnqylcd_1<zrnqylcd_2<\cdots<zrnqylcd_{2wpsdhjkv}$, we notice that there is a unique path ofiubnzr containing $(zrnqylcd_i,2+(-1)^i),(zrnqylcd_1,2)$ for $i=1,2,\ldots,2wpsdhjkv$.  This establishes the desired bijection.\par

Any path ofiubnzr in yclvxgme contains either the line segment mbvxyqre between $(ftgzlosy-1,1)$ and $(ftgzlosy,1)$, or the line segment csnlgkfa between $(ftgzlosy,2)$ and $(ftgzlosy,1)$. (This construction is reversible, lengthening any path in yclvxgme_{ftgzlosy-1} to a path in yclvxgme.) In the latter case, ofiubnzr contains the subpath csnlgkfa' which joins $(ftgzlosy-1,3)$, $(ftgzlosy,3)$, $(ftgzlosy,2)$, $(ftgzlosy,1)$ consecutively; deleting csnlgkfa' results in a path in skoajwqu_{ftgzlosy-1}, and this construction is also reversible. The desired bijection follows.\par Similarly, there is a bijection between skoajwqu and yclvxgme_{ftgzlosy-1} \cup skoajwqu_{ftgzlosy-1} for $ftgzlosy \geq 2$. It follows by induction that for $ftgzlosy \geq 2$, $|yclvxgme| = |skoajwqu| = 2^{ftgzlosy-2} (|A_1| + |B_1|)$. But $|A_1| = 0$ and $|B_1| = 1$, and hence the desired answer is $|yclvxgme| = 2^{ftgzlosy-2}$.\par

Richard Stanley provides the reference: K.L. Collins and L.B. Krompart, The number of Hamiltonian paths in a rectangular grid, 	extit{Discrete Math.}  	extbf{169} (1997), 29--38. This problem is Theorem~1 of that paper; the cases of $4 	imes ftgzlosy$ and $5 	imes ftgzlosy$ grids are also treated. The paper can also be found online at the URL 	exttt{kcollins.web.wesleyan.edu/vita.htm}.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  n \geq  2  be an integer and consider the 3 \times  n grid graph
        G_n = { (c , r) | 0 \leq  c \leq  n-1  and  r \in  {0,1,2} },
whose edges join any two vertices whose taxicab (Manhattan) distance is 1.
The rows are numbered 0 (bottom), 1 (middle), 2 (top).

A Hamiltonian path of G_n that starts in the upper-left corner and ends in the upper-right corner,
        v_1 = (0,2),      v_{3n} = (n-1,2),
will be called a rook-tour of G_n.

Determine, for every n \geq  2, the number of rook-tours of G_n.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we keep the numbering 0 (bottom), 1 (middle), 2 (top).

1.  Notation
   ----------
   For n \geq  2 write
        A_n  = { rook-tours that start at (0,2) and finish at (n-1,2) },
        B_n  = { Hamiltonian paths that start at (0,2) and finish at (n-1,0) }.
   Put a_n = |A_n| and b_n = |B_n|.  We shall prove

             a_n = b_n = 2^{n-2}            for every  n \geq  2 .

2.  The case n = 2
   ----------------
   All Hamiltonian paths on the 3 \times  2 board can be listed easily.  One finds
     A_2 = { (0,2)(0,1)(0,0)(1,0)(1,1)(1,2) },
     B_2 = { (0,2)(1,2)(1,1)(0,1)(0,0)(1,0) }.
   Hence a_2 = b_2 = 1, so the desired formula holds for n = 2.

3.  Splitting the tours in A_n   (n \geq  3)
   -------------------------------------
   The last edge used by every path in A_n must be incident with the terminal
   vertex (n-1,2); hence it is either

     Type H  (horizontal) : (n-2,2) - (n-1,2), or
     Type V  (vertical)   : (n-1,1) - (n-1,2).

   3\cdot 1  Paths of type H
        ---------------
        After the horizontal edge (n-2,2)-(n-1,2) is chosen, the other two
        vertices of the last column, (n-1,1) and (n-1,0), still have to be
        covered.  This can be done only through the 3-edge detour

                (n-2,0) - (n-1,0) - (n-1,1) - (n-2,1).        (\dagger )

        Delete the final horizontal edge and the detour (\dagger ) and insert instead
        the single edge (n-2,1)-(n-2,0).  What remains is a Hamiltonian path
        on the first n-1 columns that still starts at (0,2) and now ends at
        (n-2,2); i.e. an element of A_{n-1}.  The operation is reversible, so

              { type-H paths in A_n }  \leftrightarrow   A_{n-1}.            (1)

   3\cdot 2  Paths of type V
        ---------------
        Suppose the last edge is the vertical edge (n-1,1)-(n-1,2).  Then the
        two remaining vertices of the last column must be joined in the only
        way still possible:

                (n-1,1) - (n-1,0) - (n-2,0).                    (\ddagger )

        Deleting the fragment (\ddagger ) together with the final edge and deleting
        the last column gives a path that now ends at (n-2,0), i.e. an element
        of B_{n-1}.  Reversing the steps yields a bijection

              { type-V paths in A_n }  \leftrightarrow   B_{n-1}.             (2)

   Combining (1) and (2) we get the first recursion

                   a_n = a_{n-1} + b_{n-1}.                     (3)

4.  An analogous splitting of B_n
   ------------------------------
   For B_n the terminal vertex is (n-1,0).  Its last edge is either

        H'  (horizontal) : (n-2,0) - (n-1,0),
        V'  (vertical)   : (n-1,1) - (n-1,0).

   4\cdot 1  Type H'
        -------
        With the bottom horizontal edge fixed, the two vertices above the
        endpoint can be visited only through the upper 3-edge detour

                (n-2,2) - (n-1,2) - (n-1,1) - (n-2,1).        (\dagger \dagger )

        Deleting the final edge together with (\dagger \dagger ) and inserting the edge
        (n-2,1)-(n-2,2) yields a path in A_{n-1}.  The construction is
        reversible, hence

              { type-H' paths in B_n }  \leftrightarrow   A_{n-1}.            (4)

   4\cdot 2  Type V'
        -------
        Now the last edge is the vertical edge (n-1,1)-(n-1,0).  Because
        (n-1,0) is an endpoint, the horizontal edge (n-2,0)-(n-1,0) is absent.
        To give every vertex its required degree we are forced into the
        vertical staircase

                (n-2,2) - (n-1,2) - (n-1,1) - (n-1,0).        (\ddagger \ddagger )

        Remove the three vertices (n-1,2), (n-1,1), (n-1,0) together with the
        three staircase edges.  What remains is a Hamiltonian path on the
        first n-1 columns which still starts at (0,2) and now ends at
        (n-2,2); i.e. an element of A_{n-1}.  The operation is reversible (to
        reconstruct the staircase, append the edges in the order (n-2,2)-
        (n-1,2)-(n-1,1)-(n-1,0)), whence

              { type-V' paths in B_n }  \leftrightarrow   A_{n-1}.            (5)

   From (4) and (5) we obtain the second recursion

                   b_n = 2 a_{n-1}.                            (6)

5.  Solving the recursions
   -----------------------
   We already know a_2 = b_2 = 1.  Suppose for some n \geq  3 we have shown
   a_{n-1} = b_{n-1}.  Then (3) and (6) give

        a_n = a_{n-1}+b_{n-1} = 2 a_{n-1},
        b_n = 2 a_{n-1} = a_n.

   Thus a_n = b_n for this n as well, and by induction

        a_n = b_n  for every n \geq  2.                             (7)

   From (3) and (7) we obtain the simple first-order recursion

        a_n = 2 a_{n-1}   (n \geq  3).

   With a_2 = 1 we finally have

        a_n = 2^{n-2}   (n \geq  2),

   and the same formula for b_n by (7).

6.  Answer
   -------
   The number of rook-tours of the 3 \times  n grid graph that start at (0,2) and end
   at (n-1,2) equals

                     2^{n-2}.

   \blacksquare

core_steps: Associate to every rook tour the set S of column indices where the path enters the middle row by a vertical edge.
Observe that S must (a) contain the last column and (b) have even cardinality (entry–exit parity in the middle row).
Prove the map  Tour → S is bijective by giving an explicit reconstruction of the tour from any admissible S.
Count admissible sets: choose any even-sized subset of {1,…,n} that contains n, giving 2^{n-2} possibilities.

slot1.description: Exact coordinates of the prescribed start/end cells—as long as both are in the same extreme row, one at column 1 and the other at column n.

slot2.description: Column that is forced to lie in S because it contains an endpoint.

slot3.description: Required parity of |S| (changes if the two endpoints lie in different rows).

slot4.description: Numerical labels chosen for the three rows; any consistent triple works.
